summary:
The wall paintings and plaster reliefs found at [[event:11|Çatalhöyük]] in central Anatolia represent some of the most elaborate and well-preserved examples of prehistoric interior decoration anywhere in the world. Dating from approximately 7000 to 6000 BCE, these paintings adorned the interior walls of individual houses and were repeatedly replastered and repainted over generations, creating layered palimpsests of imagery. Subjects include naturalistic hunting scenes, geometric patterns, hand prints, and representations of wild animals — particularly aurochs, deer, and leopards — alongside depictions of vultures descending on headless human figures, which may be connected to mortuary and funerary practices observed at the site.

The paintings were applied to white lime-plaster walls using mineral pigments, primarily ochres for red and yellow tones and manganese oxide for black. Some of the most dramatic compositions depict coordinated hunting scenes in which multiple human figures surround and attack large bovids, a form of narrative imagery that stands as one of the earliest examples of pictorial storytelling. These scenes may reflect actual hunting practices, ritual or ceremonial hunts, or mythological narratives — the interpretation remains contested among scholars.

The artistic traditions of Çatalhöyük reflect a rich symbolic world in which the boundary between domestic and ritual space was fluid. Houses served simultaneously as residences, storage areas, and burial sites, with paintings, sculpted bucolrania (bull skulls fixed to walls), and [[event:1466|bull cult]] installations integrated into daily living spaces. This fusion of the domestic and the sacred gives the site's art a distinctive character that offers rare insight into the cosmological beliefs of early Neolithic Anatolian communities.
